[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an unprivileged attacker who has been granted isAuthorized[maker][attacker]=true via Midnight.setIsAuthorized call SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, maliciousRoot, true) to ratify an arbitrary Merkle root on behalf of maker, then construct a valid Merkle proof for a crafted Offer with maxUnits=type(uint256).max and tick=MAX_TICK, and take that offer to drain maker's loan tokens, violating the invariant that offer ratification must reflect maker's actual intent? Proof idea: fuzz (maker, attacker, root, offer fields) and assert that setIsRootRatified succeeds only when the caller is maker or explicitly authorized, then assert that a ratified root cannot authorize offers the maker never signed.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsRootRatified + Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator A (isAuthorized[maker][A]=true) call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(B, true, maker) to grant B authorization for maker, then B calls SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) to ratify a root that maker never approved, creating an unbounded authorization chain that bypasses maker's offer intent? [File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an authorized operator front-run a take by calling SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) to revoke a previously ratified root mid-flight, causing the take to revert with NotRatified and permanently freezing maker's offers in that Merkle tree, violating the invariant that valid offers must remain takeable until expiry or cancellation? [File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsRootRatified + Midnight.setIsAuthorized (coarse authorization)] Can an attacker who is authorized by maker (isAuthorized[maker][attacker]=true) call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(SetterRatifier, false, maker) to de-authorize the SetterRatifier contract from maker's authorization mapping, causing all of maker's existing offers using SetterRatifier to revert at the isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier] check in take, effectively freezing all maker offers without maker's consent? [File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker deploy a contract that mimics the MIDNIGHT address and calls SetterRatifier.isRatified directly with a crafted Offer and ratifierData, bypassing the NotMidnight check if MIDNIGHT is set to a predictable or CREATE2-derivable address, to learn whether a root is ratified without triggering a take? [File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsRootRatified + HashLib.isLeaf] Can an attacker supply a crafted (root, leafIndex, proof) tuple where leafIndex=0 and proof=[] such that root == HashLib.hashOffer(offer), ratifying a single-offer tree with no proof elements, and then take that offer even though the maker only intended to ratify a multi-offer Merkle tree with a different root?
